title: Exciton-mediated optical control of liquid-solid friction
authors: Timur Pryadilin, Alexey Kavokin, Baptiste Coquinot
abstract: Interfacial friction in nanofluidic systems can arise from fluctuation-induced coupling between liquid charge fluctuations and the internal excitations of the confining solid. Here, we develop a microscopic theory of exciton-mediated solid-liquid friction based on the coupling between optically generated excitons and charge fluctuations in water. We distinguish between static excitons, localized by disorder or functionalization, and dynamic excitons, which interact with water through polarization fluctuations. In both cases, we derive analytical formulas for the excitonic friction, which is experimentally tunable and can significantly reduce the slip length and thereby the hydraulic permeability of nanochannels. Applying our framework to carbon nanotubes, we quantitatively reproduce the recent measurements of Kistwal et al., showing a reduction of nanotube diffusion under optical excitation, without fitting parameters. More broadly, our results establish excitons as a mechanism to optically control nanofluidic transport and suggest that excitonic photoluminescence could provide an optical probe of flow velocity inside nanochannels.
summary: This paper proposes a way to use light to control how liquids flow through tiny channels by manipulating the friction between the liquid and the channel walls via excitons. It provides a quantitative theory that explains how optical excitation can reduce the diffusion of nanotubes in water, suggesting that light can act as a remote-control switch for nanofluidic transport.
main_problem: Understanding and controlling the mechanism of solid-liquid friction at the nanoscale using optical excitation, specifically how excitons can tune the friction between water and solid nanochannels.
main_result: The authors develop a microscopic theory showing that optically generated excitons (both static and dynamic) increase interfacial friction, which can be used to optically control nanofluidic transport and probe flow velocity.
method: The study uses a microscopic theoretical framework based on perturbation theory, Keldysh formalism, and the spectral overlap of exciton and water response functions, combined with Slender Body Theory for hydrodynamics.
model_or_system: The system consists of liquid (water) interacting with solid nanochannels, specifically carbon nanotubes. The model includes two exciton types: static (localized with permanent dipoles) and dynamic (mobile, polarized by electric fields).
key_observables: Interfacial friction coefficient, slip length, hydraulic permeability, nanotube diffusion coefficient, and excitonic photoluminescence.
important_parameters: Exciton density, nanotube radius, exciton effective mass, transition energy, water response function, and slip length.
main_assumptions: Assumes low exciton density (Boltzmann limit), uses a planar approximation for the nanotube response, and assumes the water response is radially symmetric.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows sketches of friction mechanisms for static and dynamic excitons and the exciton response function. Fig 2 illustrates exciton-mediated permeance and compares predicted slip length and membrane permeance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nanofluidic control, develops the microscopic theory for static and dynamic exciton-mediated friction, validates the model against experimental diffusion data, and discusses implications for optical probing of flow.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Sketch of fluctuation-induced friction with static (pinned) excitons having a static dipole moment. b) Sketch of fluctuation-induced friction with dynamic excitons that have zero static dipole moment (represented with gray), but can be polarized by the liquid-induced electric field. c) Response function gex(q, ω) of dynamic excitons. The red dashed line corresponds to transition from excitonic ground state, ℏω = Eα q −E0 0. The white arrow marks the region of dominant spectral weight of the water response function. Exciton and nanotube parameters are: ∆E = 20 meV, M = me, n1D ex = 106 cm−1, |d01| = 1 nm, a = 0.39 nm (single-walled CNT with chirality (6,5)). d) Friction...

FIG. 2. a) Sketch of the exciton-mediated permeance through a membrane made of nanotubes. b) Total slip length and membrane permeance (assuming 50% packing fraction) as a function of linear density of excitons. Dynamic excitons (red) have |d01| = 1 nm, M = 2me, ∆E = 20 meV. Nanotube radius and length are a = 0.39 nm and L = 1 µm. Non-excitonic slip length inside the nanotubes is b0 = 2 µm. c) Sketch of the experiment of Kistwal et al.
